candidate_problem: Mechanistic unidentifiability of higher-order interactions (HOIs) in ecological time-series data
evidence_spans: higher-order Lotka–Volterra dynamics can, in some scenarios, be accurately reproduced by effective pairwise models fitted to the same abundance time series
higher-order interactions cannot, in general, be inferred from time-series data alone
distinct interaction mechanisms generate nearly indistinguishable dynamics, potentially leading to accurate yet misleading ecological interpretations
problem_evidence_spans: standard tools of model comparison and goodness of fit are silent about mechanistic identifiability
the inclusion of higher-order terms almost always improves explanatory power in a statistical sense
even under these idealized conditions, can the presence or absence of HOIs always be deduced from abundance dynamics alone?
feasibility_evidence_spans: Using second-order Lotka-Volterra systems as our general framework, we generated in silico, high-resolution time series of species abundances
inferred a standard pairwise Lotka-Volterra model from the same data by evaluating the per capita growth rates from these trajectories and fitting them to a hyperplane
auto_research_experiment: Develop a regularization-based inference framework that penalizes HOI terms unless they provide a statistically significant improvement in out-of-distribution (OOD) forecasting compared to effective pairwise models across varying perturbation regimes.
available_data_or_benchmark: The 'BioSIM' or 'Ecological-Dynamics' datasets on GitHub, or the 'MC-GLV' (Multispecies Community Generalized Lotka-Volterra) simulator.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/stefanoalleina/GLV_simulator
scientific_hypothesis: HOIs leave a unique signature in the response to external perturbations (e.g., species removal or nutrient pulses) that is not captured by effective pairwise models fitted to steady-state or unperturbed trajectories.
expected_metric: Mean Absolute Scaled Error (MASE) on post-perturbation trajectories.
success_threshold: HOI-aware models must outperform effective pairwise models by >20% in MASE during the 10 time-steps following a species deletion event.
failure_condition: Effective pairwise models achieve equivalent predictive accuracy to HOI models even under high-magnitude perturbations.
first_24h_experiment: Simulate a 3-species system with known 2nd-order HOI terms; fit a standard pairwise GLV to the trajectory; compare the Jacobian of the fitted pairwise model vs. the true HOI model at the equilibrium point.
specific_intervention: Introduce a 'structural sensitivity' penalty in the loss function that measures the stability of inferred parameters under leave-one-species-out cross-validation.
baseline: Standard Least Squares fitting of Generalized Lotka-Volterra (GLV) equations.
metric: Parameter Identifiability Score (PIS) and OOD Forecast Accuracy.
story_angle: Ecologists might be seeing 'ghost' interactions—mathematical artifacts that fit the data but don't exist in nature—or missing real ones hidden in pairwise averages.

INTRODUCTION: Safety evaluation for large language models proceeds by scoring responses to benchmark prompts and treating those scores as evidence about deployment behavior.
This workflow presumes context robustness: behavior elicited by benchmark-style prompts is informative about behavior under analogous requests in ordinary use.

candidate_problem: Euclidean distance-based connectivity fails to account for landscape resistance and barriers in species dispersal modeling.
evidence_spans: The model can be improved by incorporating more functional definitions of connectivity. Indeed, we defined connectivity as a function of Euclidean distance between sites.
Integrating a resistance-based formulation of connectivity would be particularly relevant for species like the otter which primarily disperse along rivers and coastlines
highways and large rivers have been shown to limit distributions for many large carnivores
problem_evidence_spans: Euclidean distance alone cannot capture... highways and large rivers have been shown to limit distributions
feasibility_evidence_spans: Euclidean distance is often replaced in connectivity analyses by functional metrics like least-cost path (Coulon et al., 2015; Sutherland et al., 2015) or commute-time (McRae et al., 2008; Kervellec et al., 2024) distances
auto_research_experiment: Replace the Euclidean distance kernel in the provided dynamic occupancy model with a least-cost path (LCP) distance kernel derived from a landscape resistance surface (e.g., land cover, road density).
available_data_or_benchmark: French National Wolf Monitoring data (Louvrier et al. 2018) and Corine Land Cover (CLC) for resistance surfaces.
public_training_or_eval_data: https://github.com/oliviergimenez/wolf_occ_monit (Example of similar wolf occupancy data/code)
scientific_hypothesis: A resistance-based connectivity model will show higher predictive accuracy for wolf range expansion than a Euclidean model by correctly identifying highways as dispersal barriers.
expected_metric: WAIC (Watanabe-Akaike Information Criterion) or AUC on out-of-sample temporal colonization events.
success_threshold: A reduction in WAIC > 10 compared to the Euclidean baseline.
failure_condition: The LCP model shows no improvement in WAIC or results in biologically implausible dispersal parameters (sigma).
first_24h_experiment: Download the Corine Land Cover dataset for France, generate a 10km x 10km resistance grid where 'Highways' have 100x resistance, and compute a sparse LCP distance matrix for the wolf study area.
specific_intervention: Implementation of a Least-Cost Path (LCP) distance algorithm within the sparse matrix framework of the dynamic occupancy model.
baseline: The Euclidean distance-based dynamic occupancy model described in the paper.
story_angle: Moving from 'as the crow flies' to 'as the wolf runs'—improving ecological forecasting by mapping the actual friction of the landscape.
